title: Supporting the goals and ideals of "National Youth HIV/AIDS Awareness Day".
plain_summary: This resolution supports a special day to raise awareness about HIV and AIDS among young people. It says that young people, especially African-American youth and gay youth, are getting HIV at high rates and need better help. The resolution asks for more money for HIV prevention programs and better health services that are friendly to young people. It also wants to remove unfair laws that punish people for having HIV.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution supports the goals and ideas of National Youth HIV/AIDS Awareness Day.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: National Youth HIV/AIDS Awareness Day is a day when people across the country work to improve the health, education, and leadership of young people. More than forty years after HIV/AIDS first appeared, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention says that more than one million one hundred eighty-nine thousand seven hundred people in the United States are living with HIV. In twenty twenty, thirty thousand six hundred thirty-five people were newly diagnosed with HIV. Young people between ages thirteen and twenty-four made up twenty percent of all new HIV cases in twenty twenty. Young people with HIV are less likely than older people to stay in medical care and control their virus levels. Fifty-six percent of young people with HIV between ages thirteen and twenty-four do not know they have the virus. African-American youth are hit hardest by HIV, making up fifty-four percent of new cases in people ages thirteen to twenty-four. Young African-American gay and bisexual men are affected even more severely, representing fifty-three percent of new HIV cases among young gay and bisexual men. Young gay and bisexual men overall made up eighty-four percent of all new HIV cases in people aged thirteen to twenty-four in twenty twenty. The National HIV/AIDS Strategy shows that young people have worse outcomes when it comes to knowing their HIV status, getting prevention medicine, and staying healthy. The strategy says children and young adults with HIV need special medical care and support services as they grow up. The Division of Adolescent and School Health is the only federal program that helps prevent HIV in schools. The largest federal program for HIV care and treatment is named after Ryan White, a teenager from Indiana who helped teach the country about HIV and AIDS in the nineteen eighties. The Ryan White Part D Program helps connect young people with HIV to medical care and support. The Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act gives young people better access to health care coverage, more insurance choices, more money for sex education, protection from being denied care because of HIV, and expanded Medicaid coverage. April tenth of each year is recognized as National Youth HIV/AIDS Awareness Day.

sections.title: House of Representatives Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports the goals and ideas of National Youth HIV/AIDS Awareness Day. It encourages state and local governments, public health agencies, education agencies, schools, and media organizations to recognize and support this day. The House supports young people's right to education, prevention, treatment, and care, and to live without being criminalized, discriminated against, oppressed, or stigmatized. It promotes current, inclusive, culturally responsible, and medically accurate information about HIV, including pre-exposure prophylaxis (medicine taken before exposure to prevent HIV), in sex education classes to make sure all young people learn about HIV. The House supports removing HIV laws that are scientifically wrong and unfairly make it a crime for young people with HIV to engage in consensual behaviors or behaviors that have no risk of spreading the virus. It urges youth-friendly and accessible health care services, especially access to prevention and treatment medicines without needing parental permission, to help identify HIV early through voluntary routine testing and connect people to appropriate care and treatment as soon as possible. The House supports increasing funding for programs that help people affected by and living with HIV, including various CDC divisions, the Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program, Medicaid, AIDS Drug Assistance Programs, and programs that provide medical mentorship, peer support, community education, and help people transition to adult HIV care. It recommends a complete prevention and treatment plan that empowers young people, parents, public health workers, educators, faith leaders, and others to work with their communities and families to reduce violence, discrimination, and stigma toward people who share their sexual orientation or HIV status. The House calls for a generation free of HIV stigma that prioritizes youth leadership and development to ensure young people are involved in decisions about their health and well-being and creates a pathway for the next generation of HIV and AIDS doctors, advocates, educators, researchers, and other professionals. Finally, it recognizes the harmful impact of legislative efforts that restrict bodily autonomy for young people, such as restrictions on abortion and birth control access and bans on transgender health care, which negatively affect youth access to non-stigmatizing HIV prevention, education, confidential testing and treatment, and increase the risk of criminalization.
